results.title: Had to kill my favorite feature to survive Apple Review 🍎✂️ (Referral System) : OptiClear Discussion Forums | Product Hunt
results.content:
Alper Tayfur

Oof, painful but very relatable 😅

Apple’s review rules are pretty strict here — if premium digital features are unlocked in-app, they generally want that value to flow through In-App Purchase, not side-door referral rewards. Their guidelines around premium features and subscriptions make that pretty clear.

A safer path is usually:

reward with something non-premium / cosmetic

use Apple’s own offer codes or promo-style subscription discounts

or move the referral benefit outside the app experience

So yeah, shipping over perfection was probably the right call here. The feature can come back later in a form Apple is happier with.

Orhan Kilic
OptiClear

OptiClear [...] Orhan Kilic
OptiClear

OptiClear

@emre\_yilmaz\_easyparser Spot on! 🎯 Moving the whole 'reward engine' to a web dashboard seems like the most solid way to dance around those strict guidelines. It’s funny how we have to build these 'side doors' just to reward our own community, right? I'm definitely considering a web-based portal for V2 where users can manage their referrals and sync the rewards back to the app via their account.

Thanks for the support and the tactical advice—it's good to know I'm not the only one who had to perform 'emergency surgery' on their code right before launch! 🚢💻 :)))

Alper Tayfur

Oof, painful but very relatable 😅 [...] Orhan Kilic
OptiClear

OptiClear

@sk\_uxpin Spot on! 🎯 It’s incredibly frustrating when you build something purely for the community, but it clashes with their strict economic rules.

And you made a really solid point about the 'rewardless' share prompt. Maybe I was overthinking the incentive part. If OptiClear genuinely saves their Mac and clears up GBs of space, they might just share it out of pure love for the tool. I think I'll actually add a simple, clean 'Tell a Friend' button in the next update!

In your experience, do you think a simple share button works better if it's always visible on the dashboard, or maybe as a subtle prompt right after a successful cleanup?

Stan Kolotinskiy

results.title: CodeYam CLI & Memory: Comprehensive memory management for Claude Code | Product Hunt
results.content:
Image 22: CodeYam CLI & Memory CodeYam CLI & Memory

Maker

@jarmo_tuisk2The background agent has instructions and examples of what to look for. The primary goal is to look for something confusing that is likely to happen again in a future session. It doesn't always get it right, though, so we do review our rules on a regular basis. The dashboard provides good tools to help with reviewing and auditing rules so you can keep all rules clean and tidy easily.

Upvote (2)

Report

Share

4mo ago

Image 23: Jarmo Tuisk

Jarmo Tuisk

@jaredcosulich thanks for the detailed answers! the dashboard for auditing rules sounds really useful — right now we basically do manual grep through all our context files when something breaks, which is... not great. [...] Share

4mo ago

Image 48: Steffen Rehmann

Steffen Rehmann

This actually feels like a very real pain point. Anyone using Claude Code for a while has probably noticed the “same mistakes again” pattern. The idea of a background agent watching the transcripts and turning those into scoped rules is pretty clever. claude.md files getting stale is also very real, they start useful but slowly drift out of sync with how the project evolves.

I like that this is a lightweight CLI instead of some heavy platform. Feels like the kind of tool that quietly improves the dev workflow rather than trying to reinvent it. Curious to see how well it detects those confusion patterns in practice.

Upvote

Report

Share

4mo ago

First Previous12NextLast [...] the path-based rules loading is clever. we have rules scattered across CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and per-agent skill files so having them auto-load based on which files the agent is touching would save a lot of "why did it ignore that instruction" debugging.

gonna try CodeYam this week — the staleness detection alone would probably save us a few hours of confused debugging per week.

Upvote (2)

results.title: Trag: AI Code Review companion | Product Hunt
results.content:
The old playbook doesn t work. Legacy scanners rely on 20,000 brittle regex rules. They still miss critical issues. Human reviews drain time and don t scale.

results.title: Warp Code — the fastest way from prompt to production 🤠 : Warp Discussion Forums | Product Hunt
results.content:
Code review: Review open changes, ask for modifications, and line-edit code diffs in a dedicated panel

Code editing: A lightweight file viewing and editing experience in Warp with tabbed file viewing, a file tree, and syntax highlighting

Projects in Warp: Initialize projects with their own WARP.md files (compatible with Agents.MD, Claude.MD and cursor rules). You can also define agent profiles to launch agents with different default settings, and global slash commands.

I've been using Warp's agent to write several @Raycast extensions — and it's nearly overtaken my use of @Windsurf.

Give it a try — let me know if you like it!

5

47

Add a comment

### Replies

Aneesh M Bhat

Impressive launch! Warp Code looks like it’s going to be a huge productivity boost for developers.
